Were You Asked This?
Design Online Chess

Design a real-time chess platform similar to Chess.com that supports gameplay across multiple devices, move validation, matchmaking, leaderboards, and key features like move undo functionality.

Asked at:

OpenAI

Meta

Upstart

Google

Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

Online chess platforms like Chess.com let players find opponents through rating-based matchmaking, play timed real-time games with server-validated moves, and climb global leaderboards. The challenge isn't raw throughput, it's holding hundreds of thousands of stateful, ordered, two-party game sessions while keeping move latency under 200ms and clocks honest across asymmetric networks.

At interview scale, think 10M DAU and 500K concurrent games at peak, the interesting questions aren't about read/write QPS. They're about routing both players to the same game server, recovering in-memory state when that server crashes, pairing players fairly at the rating extremes where the pool is thin, and compensating for the fact that a mobile player in Singapore loses more clock time per move than a player on fiber in the same data center. Interviewers use chess to probe how you reason about stateful connections, server authority, and clock fairness, not whether you can rattle off a sharding scheme.

Community Solutions
Design Online Chess
by SurprisingSapphireIguana142
44
28
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users can find an opponent through rating-based matchmaking and start a real-time game.

Users can play chess in real time with server-validated moves and synchronized game clocks.

Users can view a global leaderboard where ratings update shortly after each game ends.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How would you scale game servers to host 500K concurrent games when both players in a game must land on the same host?

How do you keep chess clocks fair when one player has 30ms of network latency and the other has 200ms?

How do you run matchmaking at scale when players at the rating extremes have almost no peers online?

How do you guarantee move correctness across mobile players who drop connections constantly?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Chess is the textbook case for state-heavy push connections. Two players need server-pushed move and clock updates within ~200ms over the lifetime of a single sticky session. The pattern article covers exactly why stateful games favor consistent-hashed WebSocket routing over pub/sub fanout.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

Matchmaking workers must atomically claim two pending requests so no player is double-matched, and undo or draw negotiation needs server-mediated agreement. Both call for compare-and-set or Lua-script-style atomic operations, exactly what this pattern covers.

Read More
Scaling Reads

The leaderboard is the only high-read surface, read far more than written. Ratings change only at game end, so an indexed read replica plus a cached top-N is sufficient at this scale. The pattern article frames when caching helps versus when you need precomputation.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Redis

Matchmaking pools as sorted sets keyed by timeControl + ratingBucket, the unmatched-request work queue, and atomic claim via Lua scripts. Also a natural home for ephemeral presence and the leaderboard top-N cache. Not used for live game state, that lives in memory on the game server backed by Postgres.

Read More
PostgreSQL

System of record for users, ratings, games, and the move event log. The move log is what makes crash recovery work: a new game server can rebuild board and clocks from the latest snapshot plus replay. Partition the Moves table by month so cold games don't slow active queries.

Read More
ZooKeeper

Membership and routing for the game server tier. Each game server registers an ephemeral node and heartbeats. The session router watches the ring and routes both players for a gameId to the same host. When a node fails the ephemeral node disappears and affected games reassign deterministically.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

WhatsApp

1:1 stateful messaging with strict ordering, server authority, and seamless reconnection is structurally the same problem as chess gameplay. The differences are smaller than they look: chess just adds a clock and legal-move validation on top of the same ordered, idempotent, log-backed delivery.

Read
Practice
Uber

Pairing two parties from a real-time pool by some compatibility score (rating in chess, geo plus ETA in Uber) under worker contention is the same matchmaking core. Both face the thin-pool problem at the extremes and need progressive widening of acceptance criteria.

Read
Practice

Game Leaderboard

Direct overlap on the leaderboard FR. Same write pattern (event at game-complete), same read pattern (top-N plus pagination), same cache-friendliness because the data updates on a known cadence rather than continuously.

Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Both players routed to different game servers

If your load balancer puts each player wherever it picks, moves round-trip between servers, validation gets racy, and clocks desync. The unstated requirement is server-affinity per gameId. Weak candidates miss it; strong ones reach for consistent hashing on the gameId early.

Client-tracked clocks

Any design where the client decides remaining time opens the door to trivial cheating. The server is the only authority for time; clients render a local display from server-pushed clock updates. Saying "the client tracks its own timer and reports back" is a hard fail.

REST polling for the gameplay path

Reaching for HTTP polling for move delivery is a tell that the candidate hasn't internalized why this is a real-time problem. WebSocket (or an equivalent persistent push connection) isn't optional, it's the only way to hit sub-200ms opponent-move latency.

In-memory game state with no event log

A pure in-memory design works until the first crash, at which point every game on that host is gone forever. The move log in Postgres isn't there for analytics, it's there so a new owner can rebuild board and clocks after failure. If a candidate proposes Redis or in-memory state without a durable backing log, push them on recovery.

Functional Requirements
- user should be able to play real time chess game. (max 1 active game per player)
- user should be able to play the game over multiple devices.
- move validation: position, turn, timeout
- show leaderboard of top-K players
- user should be able to undo move if the other player has not played.
